Biography

https://engineering.tamu.edu/industrial/people/lntaimo

Course Description

Systems thinking process, systems of systems and the fundamental considerations associated

with the engineering of large-scale systems, or systems engineering including systems

modeling, design and the system development process.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in MATH 304; junior or senior

classification.

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

LO 1. Understand the anatomy of engineered systems and their complex

interactions.

LO 2. Formulate, analyze, and interpret issues associated with engineered

systems.

LO 3. Use systems thinking techniques and software tools necessary for

systems engineering

practice.

LO 4. Model and analyze engineered systems using systems engineering

tools.

Grading Policy

The graded items for the course are as follows:

Assignment  Due Date Topics Covered Percent Grade

Quizzes Weekly 10%

Exam 1  Sep 29th Modules 1, 2, and 3 15%

Exam 2  Oct 29th Modules 4 and 5 25%

Exam 3  (Final)  Dec 15th Modules 5, 6, 7 and 8 25%

Course Project Dec 9th  25%

Letter grades will be assigned as follows: A for 90% - 100%, B for 80% - 89.9%, C for 70% -

79.9%, D for 60% - 69.9%, F for below 60%. This course covers standard material on systems

engineering and there is usually no curve on grades. Any disagreements regarding a grade

received on any graded material must be discussed within two business days of the return of

the graded material. No grade will be changed beyond the two business days limit. Scores will

be maintained in the Gradebook within Canvas.

The exams will be conducted in the classroom (ETB 1027) in person. The student is required

to abide by the Aggie Honor Code. The format of the exams is closed-book (closed

everything!), multiple choice, and short-answer questions. Graded tests will not be returned

to the students; however, students can request to go over their test answers with the

instructor during office hours of the week following posting of the exam scores.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

No late work (e.g., submitting a quiz assignment after the established deadline) will be

accepted. Late work will be assigned a score of zero .

Additional Course Information

Course Project

Students will be placed in teams of three to five students, and each team will work on a

challenging real-life societal problem. The team will apply the systems engineering approach

to model, analyze and design a system model to address the problem. Student teams will

organize their own weekly meetings. The team will select one (1) problem out of three (3)

problems from the Course Project assignment (available on Canvas) based on their research

interests. The team shall write a proposal (Proposal Template available on Canvas) subject to

instructor approval. The project work will culminate into a final report that will be submitted

for grading at the end of the semester. Students will learn to use systems modeling software

(available online) to develop their systems engineering documents (Unified Modeling

Language - UML) for the project.

Software

For Module 7, students will find and learn to use a freely available online Unified Modeling

Language (UML) systems modeling software.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance is required.

AI Statement

AI text generators (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class

without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution.
